title: The Fifth Sunday of Easter
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
Sermon Date: May 2, 2021

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

May 2, 2021 was the Fifth Sunday of Easter in the Christian calendar, falling within the Easter season and positioned 39 days after Easter Sunday (April 4, 2021). Under the Revised Common Lectionary, the readings would have included Acts 7:55-60 (the martyrdom of Stephen), Psalm 31:1-5, 15-16, 1 Peter 2:2-10, and John 14:1-14 (Jesus's words about the Father's house and remaining in him), with the Gospel emphasizing Christ's promise of continued presence through the Holy Spirit. This Sunday sits in the period between Easter and Pentecost (May 23, 2021), a liturgically significant time of resurrection reflection and anticipation of the Spirit's outpouring.

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church in May 2021 was in the midst of the pandemic's lingering effects on congregational worship, with many dioceses still operating under COVID-19 protocols established in spring 2020. The Diocese of Colorado, which includes Saint John's Cathedral (the seat of Bishop Robert Willis O'Neill), was in the process of reopening and adapting worship practices as vaccination rates increased across Colorado. There were no major General Convention actions or bishop elections occurring at this exact moment, though the church was navigating broader national conversations about racial justice and LGBTQ+ inclusion that had intensified in 2020.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was experiencing a notably warm and dry late spring, with temperatures in the low 80s Fahrenheit during the last week of April leading into early May, contributing to increased wildfire concerns across Colorado (the state had already experienced significant fire seasons in 2020 and was preparing for another active season). The Denver metro area was actively reopening indoor dining and other venues as Colorado lifted many COVID-19 restrictions in late April, with a significant portion of the population vaccinated but public mask mandates still in effect in some settings. The Capitol Hill neighborhood and downtown Denver were seeing increased foot traffic and activity as the economy reopened, though many businesses were still recovering from pandemic closures.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

President Biden had completed his first 100 days in office (which ended April 30, 2021) with major infrastructure and pandemic-response initiatives underway; vaccination rates were accelerating nationwide with roughly 40% of Americans having received at least one dose by early May. The conviction of former Minneapolis police officer Derek Chauvin in the murder of George Floyd (April 20, 2021) was still dominating national conversation about policing and racial justice, with ongoing protests and discussions about police reform. The economy was showing strong signs of recovery with unemployment dropping and job openings increasing, though inflation concerns were beginning to emerge; meanwhile, the Colonial Pipeline ransomware attack and fuel shortage on the East Coast (May 7-11) was imminent and would dominate headlines within days.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The second wave of COVID-19 was devastating India, with hospitals overwhelmed and oxygen shortages creating a humanitarian crisis; on May 1-2, India was recording over 400,000 cases daily and the international community was mobilizing emergency aid. The Israeli-Palestinian conflict had recently escalated significantly with violent clashes in Jerusalem and Gaza beginning around April 23, with air strikes and rocket fire intensifying through late April and early May. The European Union and other Western nations were maintaining pressure on Russia over election security and military movements near Ukraine, though this had not yet reached the crisis point it would in 2022.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

The United States in early May 2021 inhabited a cautiously optimistic cultural moment: vaccination progress had created genuine hope that "normal life" might resume, yet underlying anxieties about the pandemic, racial justice, and political polarization remained acute. The NBA and NHL were in active playoff seasons (NBA Finals would begin May 29), and entertainment offerings were expanding as venues reopened, though many people remained uncertain about gathering indoors. There was a palpable tension between relief and residual trauma—people were eager to return to pre-pandemic activities yet haunted by loss and social division, making this an emotionally complex moment for pastoral ministry and spiritual reflection on resurrection and renewal.
